decks.title: Core Concepts
decks.description: Key distinctions that matter before choosing any algorithm.

cards.back: Encryption is meant to protect confidentiality with a key. Encoding only changes representation for transport or storage.
Hashing creates a fixed-size digest and is designed to be one-way. It is used for integrity checks, fingerprints, and related workflows.
HMAC combines a secure hash with a shared secret key to verify integrity and origin.
Authenticated encryption protects confidentiality and detects tampering in one workflow. Examples include AES-GCM and ChaCha20-Poly1305.
The same secret key is used to encrypt and decrypt. This is fast and practical for larger data.
A public/private key pair is used. It is helpful for key exchange and signatures, but slower for bulk data.

decks.title: Practical Algorithm Selection
decks.description: Short reminders for choosing the right family of tools.

cards.front: Best default for new file encryption
cards.back: Prefer AES-GCM or ChaCha20-Poly1305. Fernet is also a good high-level choice when you want a simpler API.
Use Base64 when you need a text-safe representation of binary data, not when you need secrecy.
Legacy algorithms may still appear in old systems, but they should not be selected for new designs.
Even strong algorithms fail in practice if the key is lost, reused unsafely, or exposed to the wrong people.
Compare output characteristics, timing, and the type of security guarantee each algorithm provides, not just whether it produces text.
Treat historical ciphers as learning tools. Their main value is understanding how modern cryptography improved on them.
